exercises.name: The 30 Second Rule
exercises.rule: Complete the point in 30 seconds. No padding.
exercises.why: Forces ruthless prioritization — the muscle of saying just enough.
exercises.objective: Deliver a complete, prioritized point inside a hard 30-second budget.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if rep duration exceeds 30s, conciseness MUST reflect the over-budget delivery; primary feedback bullet should name the wall-clock breach.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry cuts the warm-up and secondary detail so the complete point lands inside the 30-second budget.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate status-update and briefing scenarios with a named audience and real stakes, answerable completely in 30 seconds without research or specialized knowledge. The situation must contain more material than the budget allows so ruthless prioritization is forced. Keep each prompt to one sentence.

prompts.text: Give a 30-second status on the platform migration project.
Update the team on where the Q3 fundraise stands.
Brief your manager on yesterday's customer escalation.
Recap the all-hands meeting for someone who missed it.
Explain why the deploy got rolled back last night.
Walk me through where the patient intake redesign stands.
Update the board on this quarter's burn rate.
Status report on the curriculum rollout to the new schools.
Tell investors how the pilot with the hospital system is going.
Brief the sales team on what changed in the new pricing model.
Recap the post-mortem on the database outage.
Where are we on the holiday marketing campaign?
Brief the CEO on the legal review of the partnership deal.
Give the FDA-submission status update for the new device trial.
Update the family on grandma's hospital situation.
Brief the press on the security incident before the article runs.

exercises.name: Kill the Filler
exercises.rule: Zero filler words. Um, uh, like, basically = restart.
exercises.why: Filler is the audible sound of you thinking. Train the silence instead.
exercises.objective: Speak fluently from first word to last with zero filler words.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: filler words (um, uh, like, basically, literally) count against conciseness. Each filler over 2/min should drag conciseness toward the floor.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry replaces every filler impulse with a silent pause instead of an audible um, uh, like, or basically.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate personal, reflective storytelling prompts where thinking out loud would naturally produce filler. The topic must be answerable from lived experience with no preparation, so the only challenge is speaking without um, uh, like, or basically. Keep each prompt to one conversational sentence.

prompts.text: Tell me about a time you failed.
Describe your proudest accomplishment.
What's a hobby you've picked up recently?
Share a piece of feedback that changed how you work.
Describe a coworker you didn't get along with and why.
Talk about the hardest layoff conversation you've had.
Explain a financial mistake you made in your twenties.
Describe the moment you knew the startup was failing.
Tell me about a clinical decision you second-guessed.
Talk about a creative project you abandoned.
Describe a time a student really struggled in your class.
Tell me about a relationship that ended badly.
Pitch the company to a VC you know is skeptical.
Deliver bad news to a patient's family in the waiting room.
Address the team after the founder was just fired.
Eulogize a mentor in front of a packed room.

exercises.name: No Hedging
exercises.rule: Every hedge word banned. Full commitment only.
exercises.why: Hedges leak confidence and inflate length. Commit or cut.
exercises.objective: Commit to a clear position without hedge words that dilute conviction and inflate length.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: hedge words (might, kinda, maybe, sort of, I think) count against both conciseness and thinking_quality (low conviction).
exercises.retry_objective: Retry restates the position with every hedge word cut and full commitment in its place.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate direct judgment questions with real stakes — was it worth it, should we do it, is it working — that strongly tempt hedging. Each prompt must have a defensible yes-or-no answer so full commitment is possible without research. Keep each prompt to one short question.

prompts.text: Was the rebrand worth it?
Is remote work better than in-office?
Should we keep the free tier?
Is the new hire working out?
Was switching to microservices a good call?
Is index investing better than picking stocks?
Should physicians unionize?
Are standardized tests still useful in admissions?
Was the acquisition a mistake?
Is the four-day work week a real productivity gain?
Should we fire the underperforming VP?
Is your cofounder the right person to run sales?
Should the company go public next year?
Is gene editing in embryos ethically defensible?
Should the founder step down as CEO?
Was killing the product line the right move for shareholders?

exercises.name: One Idea Per Response
exercises.rule: One idea only. Second idea creeps in = cut.
exercises.why: Trains restraint — listeners remember one idea, not five.
exercises.objective: Scope the answer to exactly one idea and hold that line when a second idea starts creeping in.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must develop exactly one idea. A second idea entering before the first is finished MUST drag conciseness; feedback should name the sentence where the scope broke.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry picks the single strongest idea and develops only it, cutting every sibling point that crept in.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate questions that ask for the main, biggest, or single cause of something — questions that naturally tempt a list of three. The scenario must be rich enough that multiple ideas compete, so choosing one is the visible challenge. Keep each prompt to one question.

prompts.text: What's the main reason customer churn is up?
Why did the project ship late?
What's the biggest risk to next quarter?
What's the one thing the team needs to do better?
What's the root cause of the recurring outage?
Why are nurses leaving the unit?
What's driving the gross margin compression?
Why are eighth-grade reading scores dropping?
What's the single biggest blocker on the roadmap?
Why isn't the new campaign converting?
What's the core thesis behind the new fund?
Why has your marriage felt off lately?
What's the central finding of the trial?
Why should the board back you over the activist investor?
What's the one feature that justifies the entire price hike?
Why is this the right moment to bet the company on AI?

exercises.name: The Hard Stop
exercises.rule: Stop the moment your point lands. No wrap-up.
exercises.why: The summary-of-the-summary is where conciseness dies.
exercises.objective: Recognize the exact moment the point is complete and stop without any wrap-up.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: anything spoken after the point lands — summary, restatement, wrap-up — counts against conciseness. Feedback MUST quote the sentence where the response should have ended.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry ends on the sentence that completes the point, with no summary or restatement after it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate binary decision questions — option A or option B — that can be answered with a pick, one reason, and one proof, then closed. The temptation to keep talking after the answer lands must come from the stakes, not from missing information. Keep each prompt to one short either-or question.

prompts.text: Should we use AWS or GCP?
Hire the senior candidate or the junior one?
Rent or buy?
Ship Friday or wait until Monday?
Raise prices now or wait for renewal?
Treat with antibiotics or watch and wait?
Cut the marketing budget or the engineering budget?
Keep the underperforming teacher or replace them?
Take the bigger offer or stay at the smaller firm?
Rewrite the legacy system or wrap it?
Sell the position now or hold through earnings?
Bring the difficult cofounder back or move on?
Accept the term sheet or push for a better lead?
Pull the drug from the market or wait for more data?
Settle the lawsuit or fight it in court?
Stay together for the kids or separate now?

exercises.name: Cut by Half
exercises.rule: Deliver, then deliver the same idea in half the words.
exercises.why: Reveals which words were carrying weight and which were filler.
exercises.objective: Compress a just-delivered idea into half the words without losing its meaning.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the second delivery must land the same idea in roughly half the words of the first. A version that barely shrinks, or shrinks by dropping the core meaning, MUST drag conciseness.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry tightens the compressed version further, keeping only the load-bearing words.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate explain-or-pitch prompts with enough natural material to deliver twice — once in full, once at half length. Topics must be explainable from general knowledge so the compression, not the content, is the challenge. Keep each prompt to one sentence.

prompts.text: Explain why morning routines matter.
Describe what a good manager does.
Pitch why someone should try meditation.
Explain why feedback culture matters at work.
Describe how compound interest works.
Explain what a load balancer does.
Pitch the value of preventive care.
Describe why phonics instruction matters in early reading.
Explain the company's go-to-market strategy.
Describe the artistic intent behind your latest project.
Explain how CRISPR works to a curious friend.
Pitch the case for cutting the bottom 10% of the portfolio.
Explain why your team needs to double headcount this year.
Pitch the entire investment thesis to an LP in the elevator.
Defend the surgical approach you chose to a skeptical chief.
Explain quantum entanglement to a high schooler.

exercises.name: Subject Verb Object
exercises.rule: Simple SVO sentences. No clause stacking.
exercises.why: Short sentences land. Stacked clauses lose listeners by clause three.
exercises.objective: Deliver ideas in short subject-verb-object sentences instead of stacked clauses.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: clause-stacked sentences count against conciseness. Any sentence carrying more than one dependent clause MUST be flagged in feedback with a shorter SVO rewrite.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry breaks every stacked sentence into short subject-verb-object statements.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate how-it-works and process-explanation prompts — multi-step systems and flows that naturally tempt nested clauses. The subject matter should be familiar enough to explain without notes, so sentence construction is the only challenge. Keep each prompt to one sentence.

prompts.text: Explain how our pricing tiers work.
Describe how the login flow handles password resets.
Walk through how a credit card transaction settles.
Explain how the team's on-call rotation works.
Describe how the EHR routes lab results back to the ordering doctor.
Explain how our recommendation engine ranks items.
Walk through how the new tax credit phases out.
Describe how the new grading rubric scores essays.
Explain how our supply chain moves product from factory to customer.
Walk through how the auth token gets refreshed on the client.
Explain how mRNA vaccines train the immune system.
Describe how the closing process moves from offer to keys.
Explain how Kubernetes schedules pods across nodes.
Walk through how a synthetic CDO is structured.
Describe how the proton therapy beam targets a tumor.
Explain how our distributed consensus protocol handles network splits.

exercises.name: No Throat Clearing
exercises.rule: First word is load-bearing. No so, well, basically.
exercises.why: Trains you to start strong instead of warming up out loud.
exercises.objective: Open with a load-bearing first word instead of warming up out loud.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the opening seconds are the signal. Any warm-up token (so, well, basically, okay) or preamble before the first substantive word MUST drag conciseness.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry starts on the most important word of the answer with zero preamble.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate opening-moment scenarios — kicking off a meeting, an update, an introduction — where the first sentence carries the weight. The prompt should make the audience and occasion vivid so a strong first word is clearly available. Keep each prompt to one sentence.

prompts.text: Tell me about your day.
Walk me through your resume.
Introduce yourself to the new team.
Tell me about the weekend.
Open the standup with the most important update.
Kick off the customer call with the agenda.
Open the patient handoff to the next shift.
Start the investor update with the headline.
Open the parent-teacher conference with your assessment.
Start the design review by naming the central tension.
Open the architecture review with the recommendation.
Lead the team meeting with the news everyone is waiting for.
Open the keynote in front of two thousand attendees.
Start your TED talk on a topic you're nervous about.
Open the Senate hearing testimony with your central claim.
Begin the eulogy without any warm-up.

exercises.name: The Single Sentence
exercises.rule: Entire answer in one sentence. One period only.
exercises.why: Extreme compression — surfaces the single load-bearing claim.
exercises.objective: Compress the entire answer into one sentence that carries the single load-bearing claim.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response uses more than one sentence (one period), conciseness MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.retry_objective: Retry rebuilds the sentence around the one claim that matters, cutting the stacked clauses that smuggle extras in.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate summarize, define, or state-the-thesis prompts whose material tempts a multi-sentence answer but honestly compresses into one sentence. Avoid topics that require enumeration, since lists cannot survive a one-period limit. Keep each prompt to one sentence.

prompts.text: Summarize Q3 in one sentence.
Describe your company in one sentence.
Define what your team does to a stranger at a party.
Sum up your weekend in one sentence.
Summarize the customer's complaint.
State the thesis of the research paper.
Define what a good user experience is.
Summarize the patient's chief complaint and plan.
Sum up the board's decision on the acquisition.
Define the firm's investment strategy.
Summarize the new policy for the all-hands.
State the moral of the novel you just finished.
Summarize the entire trial outcome for the press.
Define what makes your leadership style different in one line.
Sum up the war's geopolitical impact for a briefing memo.
State the company's reason to exist for the S-1 cover page.
